summary: Gruber analyzes Microsoft's Metro interface for Windows 8, arguing it represents a genuinely reimagined computing paradigm comparable to what iOS is to Apple. He initially criticized Microsoft's approach of combining Metro with the traditional Windows desktop, since iPad's success stems from the advantages of its restrictions. However, he revises his position after realizing Microsoft likely plans to run Metro-only on ARM tablets while keeping the full desktop only on Intel PCs. He notes that Metro apps have iOS-like restrictions including background suspension and no file manager, making it a credible iPad competitor when separated from legacy Windows.
key_points: Metro is Microsoft's equivalent of iOS — a clean break from legacy Windows, not an evolution of it
The iPad's success comes from mutually exclusive tradeoffs: restricting background processing enables all-day battery life and cool-running hardware
Microsoft likely plans Metro-only on ARM tablets and Metro-plus-desktop on Intel PCs, mirroring Apple's iOS/Mac OS X separation
Metro apps have iOS-like constraints: five-second suspension after leaving screen, no file manager, no explicit app quitting
Gruber amends his earlier criticism: Metro-only Windows 8 on ARM could be an excellent iPad rival, even if full Windows 8 never will be
money_quotes: Metro is to Microsoft what iOS is to Apple — starting over from scratch, carrying no legacy baggage or expectations.
The iPad's appeal and success is largely forged by the advantages that come from not allowing you to do so many of the things Mac OS X can do.
But an iPad runs for double-digit hours on a single charge with a lightweight battery and never even gets warm let alone hot.
Tradeoffs. Mutually exclusive tradeoffs. Separate devices are required.
You can't force Mac apps to follow iOS background processing restrictions without eliminating all the good and useful things we ask Macs to do in the background.
That have-your-cake-and-eat-it-too attitude is what I didn't get with Microsoft's positioning Metro as its answer to the iPad.
Windows 8 with the full Windows desktop will never be an iPad rival. But a version of Windows 8 with nothing but Metro looks like an excellent design for an iPad rival.
Isn't window an odd word choice for what we call movable, stackable, resizeable content regions in a user interface?
key_insight: The iPad succeeds because of its restrictions, not despite them, so Microsoft's only viable tablet strategy is letting Metro stand alone on ARM without the legacy Windows desktop.
